summary: Gruber responds to an OSNews headline that equated CarrierIQ's presence on Android and iOS, arguing this is a textbook case of false equivalence. On iOS, the diagnostic logging is off by default, plainly labeled, and limited to location data and call activity status. On HTC Android phones, CarrierIQ secretly logs keystrokes, SMS messages, URLs, app usage, and location with no way to disable it. Gruber defends his coverage against accusations of sudden bias, noting he consistently placed blame on carriers. He concludes that Apple's only guilt is using services from a company that others deployed for nefarious purposes.
key_points: iOS CarrierIQ logging is opt-in, limited in scope, and clearly disclosed in a readable privacy policy, while Android implementations secretly log nearly everything with no user control
Equating the two platforms' use of CarrierIQ is a textbook false equivalence that obscures the real privacy violation
The scandal is not the mere presence of CarrierIQ software but the surreptitious logging and collection of sensitive private data without user knowledge or consent
Apple's guilt is purely by association — using the same vendor that others deployed as a hidden keylogger
money_quotes: From that information, Holwerda chooses the headline 'CarrierIQ Rootkit Found on Android, iOS'.

I enjoy a debate regarding my work and any perceived biases in it, and I'd like to think that OSNews is a reasonable source with a different perspective, which is why I'm responding to this. But I worry here that I'm trying to reason with the unreasonable.
How could my stance on Carrier IQ 'suddenly' change when I'd never written about it before yesterday?
What's important here is not merely the presence of anything related to 'Carrier IQ'. What's important is the surreptitious logging and collection of sensitive private data.

There's not a shred of evidence that Apple is now or ever was using Carrier IQ for anything other than collecting only and exactly the sort of data Apple says, plainly, that it collects when the user chooses — explicitly — to allow it.

The worst that can be said of Apple in this saga is that they're guilty by association — that Apple used, for innocuous purposes, the services of a company that others have used for nefarious purposes.
To put this in the same boat as Android devices which ship from the factory with secret keyloggers installed is absurd.
key_insight: The CarrierIQ scandal was about secret, comprehensive surveillance on Android phones, and lumping Apple's disclosed, opt-in, limited diagnostics into the same story is a dangerous false equivalence that obscures the real privacy violation.
